localMaterialPolicy: Original source files and searchable extracts are gitignored. This tracked index contains verifiable hashes, locators, exact approved excerpts, rights metadata, and matching constraints.

rules: Choose one dominant source family before selecting passages.
A dominant source must have runtimeSpineAvailable=true; primarySpineEligible alone is editorial potential, not an implemented spine.
The dominant source's compatibleFamilies must include each secondary source family; compatibility is directional, not mutual.
A source with requiresExplicitOptIn is eligible only when the profile explicitly contains one of those phrases; theme similarity is insufficient.
Never insert a passage solely because a theme keyword matches.
Only passages with approvalStatus=approved and rights.runtimeEligible=true may reach generation.
Never rewrite exactText; write separate bridges when needed.

passageSchema.provenanceHashBasis: lowercase hex SHA-256 of NFC(exactText), after replacing every whitespace run with one ASCII space and trimming; UTF-8 encoded

sources.title: Seder Haggadah Shel Pesaḥ
sources.creator: Paltiel Birnbaum; Hebrew Publishing Company

rights.basis: Public-domain edition dedicated CC0 after documented non-renewal

passages.exactText: A CUP OF WINE IS PLACED BEFORE EACH PERSON AT THE TABLE. EVERYONE IS TO DRINK FOUR CUPS OF WINE DURING THE SEDER.

locator.edition: Seder Haggadah Shel Pesaḥ
locator.sourceLocation: PDF p. 15 (printed p. 13), Kadesh setup; visually verified

conditions: Credit Birnbaum, Hebrew Publishing Company, and Aharon N. Varady's digitization

attribution.creator: Paltiel Birnbaum; Hebrew Publishing Company
attribution.containingSource: Seder Haggadah Shel Pesaḥ
attribution.readerCredit: From Seder Haggadah Shel Pesaḥ by Paltiel Birnbaum; Hebrew Publishing Company.

passages.exactText: We were once the slaves of Pharaoh in Egypt, but the Lord our God brought us out from there with a mighty hand and an outstretched arm. Had not God brought our fathers out of Egypt, our children and grandchildren would still be enslaved to a Pharaoh in Egypt. Even if we all were wise and intelligent, learned and versed in the Torah, it would nevertheless be our duty to tell about the exodus from Egypt. The more anyone discusses the exodus from Egypt, the more praise he deserves.

locator.edition: Seder Haggadah Shel Pesaḥ
locator.sourceLocation: PDF p. 25 (printed p. 21), Maggid; visually verified

sources.title: The Wandering Is Over Haggadah: Including Women's Voices
sources.creator: JewishBoston and Jewish Women's Archive

conditions: Preserve attribution and ShareAlike notice
Review separately credited embedded material item by item

acquisition.localFile: research/source-materials/Wandering Is Over Including Womens Voices 2011.pdf

passages.exactText: Tonight we gather together to celebrate Passover, our holiday of freedom. We will eat a great meal together, enjoy four glasses (at least!) of wine, and tell the story of our ancestors’ liberation from slavery in Egypt. We welcome our friends and family members from other backgrounds to reflect with us on the meaning of freedom in all our lives and histories. We will consider the blessings in our lives, pledge to work harder at freeing those who still suffer, and begin to cast off the things in our own lives that oppress us.

locator.edition: The Wandering Is Over Haggadah: Including Women's Voices

conditions: Preserve attribution and ShareAlike notice
Review separately credited embedded material item by item

attribution.creator: JewishBoston and Jewish Women's Archive
attribution.containingSource: The Wandering Is Over Haggadah: Including Women's Voices
attribution.readerCredit: From The Wandering Is Over Haggadah: Including Women's Voices by JewishBoston and Jewish Women's Archive.

passages.exactText: To wash your hands, you don’t need soap, but you do need a cup to pour water over your hands. Pour water on each of your hands three times, alternating between your hands. If the people around your table don’t want to get up to walk all the way over to the sink, you could pass a pitcher and a bowl around so everyone can wash at their seats… just be careful not to spill!

sources.title: Haggadah of the Inner Seder
sources.creator: Rabbi David Seidenberg / neohasid.org

conditions: Attribute David Seidenberg and link the license
Indicate changes and preserve ShareAlike terms

passages.exactText: The seder (which means “order”) is literally an ordering of symbols so that they progress from slavery to freedom. So, for example, matsah can symbolize slavery, leaving Egypt, coming into the land, and messianic redemption. But on seder night, we first experience it as the bread of slavery (lachma anya), and end by experiencing it as the bread of redemption (afikoman). This transformation is the essence of the seder.

locator.edition: Haggadah of the Inner Seder

conditions: Attribute David Seidenberg and link the license
Indicate changes and preserve ShareAlike terms

attribution.creator: Rabbi David Seidenberg / neohasid.org
attribution.containingSource: Haggadah of the Inner Seder
attribution.readerCredit: From Haggadah of the Inner Seder by Rabbi David Seidenberg / neohasid.org.

passages.exactText: Add hyssop, known as eizov in Hebrew and za’atar in Arabic, to your karpas or seder plate, to remember the blood on the lintel that protected the firstborn, to remember that Palestinian and Israelite culture are rooted in the same land, and to hope for a future where no children are sacrificed to terror or to war. (You can also use a sprig of oregano or thyme if you cannot find hyssop.)

locator.edition: Haggadah of the Inner Seder

conditions: Attribute David Seidenberg and link the license

sources.title: The Freedom Seder for the Earth
sources.creator: Rabbi Arthur Waskow and The Shalom Center

conditions: Attribute Waskow and The Shalom Center
Preserve source-presented credits and ShareAlike notice

passages.exactText: Oppression of human beings and destruction of the earth go hand in hand. Our ancient forebears could see that there was a connection.

locator.edition: The Freedom Seder for the Earth

conditions: Attribute Waskow and The Shalom Center
Preserve source-presented credits and ShareAlike notice

attribution.readerCredit: From The Freedom Seder for the Earth by Rabbi Arthur Waskow and The Shalom Center.

passages.exactText: Why do we break this bread in two? Because if we hold on to the whole loaf for ourselves, it remains the bread of oppression. If we break it in order to share it, it becomes the bread of freedom.

sources.title: Haggadah Shir Ge'ulah / Song of Liberation
sources.creator: Emily Aviva Kapor-Mater

conditions: Credit Emily Aviva Kapor-Mater
Link the license and identify modifications

passages.exactText: We elaborate upon our story because there is nobody else to do it for us. This is our story to tell; therefore let us raise up our own voices. And when the story is not ours but that of other people, let us remember to lift up their voices so that they may tell their own stories. Let us not silence them, but let us let them speak for themselves.

locator.edition: Haggadah Shir Ge'ulah / Song of Liberation

conditions: Credit Emily Aviva Kapor-Mater
Link the license and identify modifications

attribution.creator: Emily Aviva Kapor-Mater
attribution.containingSource: Haggadah Shir Ge'ulah / Song of Liberation
attribution.readerCredit: From Haggadah Shir Ge'ulah / Song of Liberation by Emily Aviva Kapor-Mater.

passages.exactText: So far, everything we have eaten in the course of the seder has had some ritual or symbolic significance. But one of the mitzvot of the holiday is to celebrate, and one of the traditional things we do to celebrate is partake of a festive meal. The ritual nature of the food of the meal is simply that it is there to be enjoyed, which itself is a mitzvah.

locator.edition: Haggadah Shir Ge'ulah / Song of Liberation

conditions: Credit Emily Aviva Kapor-Mater
Link the license and identify modifications

sources.title: The Velveteen Rabbi's Haggadah for Pesach
sources.creator: Rabbi Rachel Barenblat

conditions: Credit Rachel Barenblat
Share freely and do not sell
Preserve printed credits for embedded material

passages.exactText: Maggid, the Hebrew word for “story,” is at the root of the word haggadah. In re-telling the story of the Exodus, we speak ourselves into our communal past.

locator.edition: The Velveteen Rabbi's Haggadah for Pesach

conditions: Credit Rachel Barenblat
Share freely and do not sell
Preserve printed credits for embedded material

attribution.creator: Rabbi Rachel Barenblat
attribution.containingSource: The Velveteen Rabbi's Haggadah for Pesach
attribution.readerCredit: From The Velveteen Rabbi's Haggadah for Pesach by Rabbi Rachel Barenblat.

passages.exactText: What does washing our hands tell us? That bodies are sacred and deserving of care. It is our hands that plant and write, that caress and create—and also our hands that strike and poison and smash. We wash our hands not to absolve ourselves of responsibility, but to affirm the need to make our hands holy. At this season of freedom and rebirth, we consecrate our hands to the task of building freedom for all who suffer.

locator.edition: The Velveteen Rabbi's Haggadah for Pesach

passages.exactText: For the rest of the seder, we read in a circle, each person reading as many lines as they desired, with leaders adding additional commentary and explanation throughout.

locator.sourceLocation: Official Open Siddur snapshot post 10879, after A Stand Against All Forms Of Oppression

passages.exactText: Passover has a strong tradition of asking questions. We encourage you to use this as an opportunity to ask the questions you are usually afraid to ask. No questions are off the table. Let this be a tool to facilitate deeper understanding between us, leaving us with a deeper commitment to each other’s struggle.

passages.exactText: Now the alternate challenge — We’re going to make our own! Going round the table, we will count up from one to thirteen, thinking of something else (preferably but not necessarily Jewish content) that fits that number. After each number, we will respond, together, singing everything we’ve come up with. By the end we’ll have to remember them all!

sources.title: The Ritual of the Seder and the Agada of the English Jews Before the Expulsion
sources.creator: Dávid Kaufmann; transcription by Aharon N. Varady

conditions: Credit Dávid Kaufmann and Aharon N. Varady
Preserve transcription license and indicate changes

passages.exactText: Jacob b. Jehuda of London, the author of that valuable contribution to the literary side of Anglo-Jewish history, the Talmudical compendium Etz Chaim, so providentially rescued and preserved for us, never dreamt, when he noted down, in the year 1287, the Ritual and Agada of the Seder Nights according to English usage, that he was fixing a permanent picture of what was doomed to destruction, and was recording not a mere portion of the liturgy, but a page of Jewish history.

locator.edition: The Ritual of the Seder and the Agada of the English Jews Before the Expulsion
locator.sourceLocation: Official Open Siddur snapshot post 4609, opening historical essay

passages.exactText: In England, all the fruits named in the Song of Songs—dates, figs, pomegranates, nuts and apples—were crushed with almonds and moistened with vinegar.

passages.exactText: They were made slaves, to labor without hope of reward, to toil without opportunity for rest.

locator.edition: Haggadah or Home Service for the Festival of Passover

conditions: Credit J. Leonard Levy and the digitization

attribution.creator: J. Leonard Levy
attribution.containingSource: Haggadah or Home Service for the Festival of Passover
attribution.readerCredit: From Haggadah or Home Service for the Festival of Passover by J. Leonard Levy.

passages.exactText: May we employ our days on earth wisely, devoting ourselves to all causes that tend to better the conditions of our fellow-men. Loving one another, aiding one another, and trusting faithfully in Thee, may we peacefully spend our allotted days on earth and look forward fearlessly to the eternal beyond.

passages.exactText: The parsley represents the spring time, renewal, the lush verdancy of our home world, Ḳobol. Also, the algae slop we have been eating for the past three years.

passages.exactText: The fourth cup is the cup of fellowship. We must always remember our responsibility to this fleet and to the survival of the human race.
